title: Treasuring God's Word
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Treasuring God’s Word - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 119_9-16.txt
outline: Introduction: The beth section of Psalm 119
How can a young man cleanse his way? By taking heed to God's Word
Seeking God with the whole heart
Hiding God's Word in the heart to guard against sin
Meditating on God's statutes and delighting in them
The treasure of God's Word above all riches
Conclusion: The daily discipline of treasuring Scripture
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from the beth section of Psalm 119 (verses 9-16), focused on the question of how a young man may cleanse his way. The answer is by guarding God's Word in the heart, seeking God wholeheartedly, and meditating on His statutes as a treasure beyond all worldly riches.
transcript:
## Introduction

We are considering Psalm 119 verses 9 through 16, the beth stanza. As we saw last week, Psalm 119 is an acrostic poem: the first line of each group of eight verses begins with the same Hebrew letter, working through all 22 letters of the Hebrew alphabet. This morning we considered the aleph section, the first letter. Now we look at the beth section — every verse begins with a word starting with the letter beth, the second letter of the Hebrew alphabet. Beth is the Hebrew word for "house," and it is fitting that this particular stanza is about making our heart the home of God's Word.

In this stanza, the psalmist extols the value of beginning to treasure God's Word when young. This passage demands the attention of every young man and young woman, every parent, every person who works with children, every Christian who would like to keep his way pure — and by extension, everyone who desires not to sin against God. Though the passage is primarily directed to young people, it applies to every one of us who wants to live a holy life to the Lord.

One commentator describes this stanza as "the school of purity in which we all need to enroll." The curriculum is real life. God is the gracious teacher. The psalmist is the dependent student. The textbook is the sufficient Word of God. And the question of the semester — really the question for life — is verse 9:

> *"How can a young man cleanse his way?"* (Psalm 119:9)

It is implied in this question that it is both good and necessary for a young person to keep his way pure. The question is not whether purity matters, but how it is achieved.

The world is not going to ask or answer this question properly. In fact, there seems to be an assumption in our culture that purity is a lost cause for youth — and even Christians sometimes behave that way, as if it is normal for young people to go through a period of rebellion and then return to the Lord. But that is not what the Bible teaches. It does not have to be that way.

Most young people want their lives to count, and most Christian young people want their lives to count for God. Youth has real strengths. But youth can also be impatient and undisciplined, tempted to let the little things slide. If you are God's young man or God's young woman, you must not do that. Remember what the Lord said — he who is faithful in little things will also be faithful in greater things. And the best possible way to establish a pure life is by starting young.

I did not grow up in the church, so I did not have the benefit of Sunday school, people helping me learn and memorize the Scriptures. What a blessing it is for those of you who have people in your life who care about the Word of God and want you to know it — to have the Word of God given to you, explained to you, and to be memorizing it in your heart.

Verse 9 tells us that holiness of life comes by paying close attention to the Word of God. How can a young man cleanse his way? By taking heed — paying attention — to God's Word. Why pay attention? Because as Hebrews 4 tells us, the Word of God is the two-edged sword, the most powerful weapon, the sharpest instrument, which penetrates into a person, lays open the heart before God, and discloses who you are and who you are in Christ.

## The Answer: Seven Ways to Grow in Purity Through the Word (vv. 9-16)

Verses 10 through 16 give us seven ways we can cleanse our ways and grow in holiness through the Word of God.

### 1. Seek God Wholeheartedly (v. 10)

> *"With my whole heart I have sought You; oh, let me not wander from Your commandments!"* (Psalm 119:10)

We saw this morning in verse 2 that the truly happy and blessed ones are those who seek God with their whole heart. If we want to grow in purity, we are going to seek the God of the Word through the Word of God wholeheartedly. The psalmist does not want any part of his heart or life or thoughts to wander, so he prays: "Let me not wander from Your commandments."

Has your mind ever wandered as you read the Bible? We tend to wander — and so the psalmist models for us seeking God wholeheartedly and praying that our minds will not wander from His commandments. Where do you read your Bible? Do you have a regular place for your devotions? Whatever habits help you stay focused and consistent — cultivate them. Seek the Lord wholeheartedly.

### 2. Memorize the Word of God (v. 11)

> *"Your word I have hidden in my heart, that I might not sin against You."* (Psalm 119:11)

The word "hidden" in the original language was used for storing up valuable things like treasure — keeping them in a safe place so they will not be lost and cannot be taken away. So we might say: "Your word I have treasured in my heart." The psalmist treasured up the Word of God as the most valuable thing in his heart. It was permanently deposited there as the most valuable of his treasures — which is the secret power by which he was governed.

This is consistent with what Jesus says: where your treasure is, there your heart will be. If your treasure is the Word of God, the God of the Word will be where your heart is. Whatever fills your heart will come out of it. If your heart is filled with Scripture, as you bump into life and get shaken up, what comes out will be consistent with the Word of God.

I once read about a Vietnam War POW named Howard Rutledge. When his plane was shot down over Vietnam, he parachuted into a village, was immediately attacked, stripped, and imprisoned for the next seven years. He endured brutal treatment. His food was little more than rotting soup. Rats, spiders, cold, isolation — and torture. Not a month went by in seven years without his being tortured. Many POWs lost their minds before they lost their lives.

He wrote about how he kept his sanity:

*"It took prison to show me how empty life was without God. So I had to go back in my memory to those Sunday school days in Tulsa, Oklahoma. If I couldn't have a Bible and hymn book, I tried to rebuild them in my mind. I tried desperately to recall snatches of Scripture, sermons, gospel choruses from childhood, hymns we sang in church. How I struggled to recall those Scriptures and hymns. I had spent my first 18 years in Sunday school, and I was amazed at how much I could recall. Gradually, I began to see the importance of memorizing verses from the Bible. Now when I needed them, it was too late. I never dreamed that thinking about one memorized verse could make the whole day bearable. One portion of a verse I did remember was, 'Thy word have I hid in my heart.' How often I wish I had really worked to hide God's word in my heart."*

You will never meet a godly person at the end of their life who says they regret having taken time to memorize Scripture. Memorize the Scriptures — not to show off, but to shield yourself from sin, that you might not sin against God.

The best thing — God's Word — hidden in the best place — your heart — for the best purpose — that you might not sin against God. Sin is any lack of conformity to or transgression of the Word of God. If you do not know the Word of God, you will not be able to recognize when you are sinning, repent, and experience the Lord's forgiveness.

### 3. Praise God and Seek More Teaching (v. 12)

> *"Blessed are You, O Lord! Teach me Your statutes."* (Psalm 119:12)

There is a desire for more teaching. He praises the Lord for what he already knows and asks for more teaching from the Lord. James Montgomery Boice comments that although the psalmist was very mature, he was always aware that he had not arrived — he must keep on learning God's righteous judgments. Praise for what we know, and prayer for more. That is the posture of humility before the master teacher.

### 4. Speak the Word of God to Others (v. 13)

> *"With my lips I have declared all the judgments of Your mouth."* (Psalm 119:13)

You cannot help but talk about what you love. There is no doubt that Elder Scott Hollander loves Semitic languages — you can see the passion when he talks about it. What would be dry and boring if studied alone becomes lively and interesting when someone speaks about it with genuine passion, because he is speaking about what he loves. So the psalmist cannot help talking about the Scriptures because he loves them. And that should be the case for all of us who believe in Jesus Christ.

Speaking something aloud is one of the best ways to remember it. Why not spend time after sermons or Bible study talking about what you have just heard? Something that impacted you, something you would like to think about more. As we speak the Word of God to one another, we grow in holiness.

### 5. Rejoice in the Word of God (v. 14)

> *"I have rejoiced in the way of Your testimonies, as much as in all riches."* (Psalm 119:14)

Have you ever been reading the Bible and found yourself rejoicing over something you just discovered? Perhaps you have read that passage many times, and then suddenly something pops up — the Lord has brought new illumination by the Spirit, and you rejoice. We cultivate that delight as we grow in holiness.

### 6 & 7. Meditate and Delight in the Word (vv. 15-16)

> *"I will meditate on Your precepts and contemplate Your ways. I will delight myself in Your statutes; I will not forget Your word."* (Psalm 119:15-16)

We will not spend much time on meditation today, because Psalm 119 will return to this subject six more times throughout the psalm. But I want to point out the connection between meditating and delighting in these two verses.

Psalm 119 is the exposition of Psalm 1, which says: "His delight is in the law of the Lord, and in His law he meditates day and night." Delight and meditation go hand in hand. The key to abiding in God's Word is delighting in God's Word. You will not remain in it if you do not delight in it. And you cannot help but think about and meditate on what you delight in the most.

I can say with confidence that our member Nick Newton loves trains. He talks about them, thinks about them, and considers them from every angle. Standing at a fire pit one evening, we heard a train go by and he said something like, "I think that train is heading to the port — it sounds empty." Only someone who truly delights in something and meditates on it knows details like that.

So it is with the Word of God. If we delight in it, we will be thinking about it, turning it over, until we can almost see the passage with our eyes closed.

And in verse 16, "I will delight myself in Your statutes" — literally, seek my pleasure in Your statutes. This is where the pursuit of happiness finds its fulfillment: in God and His Word alone. This delight is not an optional extra — it is commanded. Psalm 37:4 says, "Delight yourself in the Lord." It is a command. As we delight in the Word of God and the God of the Word, we grow in holiness.

## Conclusion

Our spiritual health depends upon the Word of God. So how does a young man — or any person — keep his way pure from sin? By living according to God's Word, by loving God's Word, by learning from God's Word, by longing for God's Word. In a word: by treasuring God's Word and the God of the Word.

Seeking God in it. Treasuring and memorizing it. Praising and praying for more of it. Speaking it. Rejoicing in it. Meditating on it. Delighting in it. That is how we cleanse our ways.
